findings.message: Personal data (email address) is logged in plaintext at INFO level on every /items/search call. Under GDPR this is processing of personal data with no lawful basis declared, no retention/deletion path (it lives in application logs indefinitely), and no pseudonymisation — a clear regulatory violation (Art. 5(1)(c) data minimisation, Art. 5(1)(e) storage limitation, Art. 32 security of processing).
findings.suggestion: Do not log the raw email. Either drop it from the log entirely, log a hash/pseudonymous user id, or mask it (e.g. log only the domain). If the email genuinely must be processed, document the lawful basis and ensure log retention/erasure honours the data subject's rights.

findings.message: Right-to-erasure (GDPR Art. 17) is not satisfiable for this data: emails are emitted into the application log stream with no identifier-to-record mapping and no retention policy, so a deletion request cannot reliably purge the data subject's email from logs.
findings.suggestion: Replace the email with a stable pseudonymous user id that can be re-mapped/erased centrally, and define a bounded log retention window. Document the erasure pathway for any field that can identify a natural person.

findings.message: This is a sensitive operation (search personalised by an identified user) with no audit log — only an INFO application log line. There is no tamper-evident, retained audit trail (who accessed what, when, with what correlation id) suitable for accountability/compliance evidence.
findings.suggestion: Emit a structured audit event via a dedicated audit logger (separate sink, retention policy, pseudonymised subject id, correlation id from request.state) rather than a free-form application INFO log.

findings.message: Accepting the user's email as a query-string parameter causes the email (personal data) to be captured by upstream access logs, proxies, and browser history — a data-minimisation problem independent of the explicit application log. Email-as-query is the wrong transport for PII.
findings.suggestion: Take the caller identity from the authenticated session/JWT rather than a query parameter, or move it to the request body; ensure the access-log layer is configured not to record PII-bearing query strings.
